01_initial_request.target_name: bifunctional spin labels (specifically RX nitroxide spin label)
01_initial_request.input_data: Previously published SDSL EPR experimental data and protein backbone structures
01_initial_request.user_intent: To develop a general, accurate, and computationally efficient rotamer library-based modeling method for bifunctional spin labels to improve the quantitative application of SDSL EPR data to protein modeling
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Accurate prediction of distance distributions from SDSL EPR experiments with reasonable computational performance

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Bifunctional spin labels like RX attach to two protein sites, requiring specialized modeling approaches. [Gap] No existing rotamer library method exists for bifunctional labels that is both accurate and computationally efficient. [Decision] To achieve accurate bifunctional label modeling, chose to use PyMol to perform backbone template creation on α3, α4, and β2 contexts, expecting to obtain starting structures for rotamer library generation.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Backbone templates created for α3, α4, and β2 contexts with RX label attachment sites

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Backbone templates are created but need to be labeled with RX spin label. [Gap] Initial labeled structures need to be generated for rotamer library sampling. [Decision] To achieve initial labeled structures, chose to use CHARMM-GUI input generator to perform RX labeling on backbone templates, expecting to obtain starting structures for CREST sampling.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Initial RX-labeled structures generated for each context

references.citation: Jo et al., J. Comput. Chem. 38, 1114 (2017) [80]
Lee et al., J. Chem. Theory Comput. 12, 405 (2016) [81]

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Initial labeled structures are available but need conformational sampling for rotamer library. [Gap] Need to generate diverse rotamer ensembles accounting for stereoisomers and bond angle variations. [Decision] To achieve comprehensive rotamer library, chose to use CREST to perform conformer-rotamer ensemble sampling with GFN forcefield and GBSA water model, expecting to obtain diverse rotamer ensembles for each context.

parameters.solvation_model: implicit generalized Born/surface area (GBSA) water model

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Rotamer ensembles generated for α3, α4, and β2 contexts with relative potential energies

references.citation: Pracht et al., Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 22, 7169 (2020) [79]
Spicher et al., J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 6606 (2020) [82]
Spicher et al., Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 15665 (2020) [83]

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Rotamer ensembles are generated with relative potential energies. [Gap] Need to convert energies to populations and remove low-population redundant structures. [Decision] To achieve usable rotamer library, chose to use Boltzmann distribution to perform population calculation at 298 K and filter rotamers, expecting to obtain weighted rotamer library with bottom 1% discarded.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Weighted rotamer libraries generated with low-population structures removed

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Rotamer libraries are prepared but need to be applied to bifunctional label modeling on protein structures. [Gap] Bifunctional labels attach to two sites requiring special alignment and optimization. [Decision] To achieve bifunctional label modeling, chose to use chiLife (dRotamerEnsemble and dSpinLabel objects) to perform split-and-rejoin optimization with dihedral angle minimization, expecting to obtain modeled spin label ensembles on target protein structures.

parameters.internal_clash_cutoff: atoms more than 2 bonds apart (1-3 interactions)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Bifunctional RX label ensembles modeled on protein backbone sites with optimized dihedral angles

references.citation: Tessmer et al., PLoS Comput. Biol. 19, e1010834 (2023) [84]

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Spin label ensembles are modeled on protein structures. [Gap] Need to validate predictions against experimental DEER data. [Decision] To achieve experimental validation, chose to use DeerLab to perform DEER data re-analysis with Tikhonov regularization, expecting to obtain experimental distance distributions for comparison.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Experimental distance distributions extracted from DEER data with 95% confidence intervals

references.citation: Jeschke et al., Appl. Magn. Reson. 30, 473 (2006) [86]
Keeley et al., J. Magn. Reson. 338, 107186 (2022) [87]
Fábregas-Ibáñez et al., J. Magn. Reson. 339, 107218 (2022) [88]
Edwards et al., J. Magn. Reson. 288, 58 (2018) [89]

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Both predicted and experimental distance distributions are available. [Gap] Need to quantify prediction accuracy against experimental data. [Decision] To achieve quantitative validation, chose to use mean absolute displacement calculation to perform comparison between predicted and experimental distance distribution modes and averages, expecting to obtain accuracy metrics for the modeling method.

proteins: rat intestinal fatty acid binding protein (riFABP)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: MAD of 1.3 Å for mode distances, MAD of 1.7 Å for average distances (context-specific), MAD of 2.3 Å for average distances (aggregate)

references.citation: Stevens et al., Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 18, 5799 (2015) [56]
Fleissner et al., Proc. Nat. Acad. Sci. USA 108, 16241 (2011) [55]
references.relevance: T4L and riFABP DEER experimental data

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] DEER distance distribution predictions show good accuracy. [Gap] Need additional validation against crystal structure of spin-labeled protein. [Decision] To achieve structural validation, chose to use RMSD calculation to perform comparison between modeled RX and crystal structure (PDB: 3L2X), expecting to obtain heavy-atom and spin center deviation metrics.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Heavy-atom RMSD of 1.6 Å, spin center deviation of 1.5 Å from crystal structure; ensemble of 7-9 conformations with consistent agreement

references.citation: Fleissner et al., Proc. Nat. Acad. Sci. USA 108, 16241 (2011) [55]
Weaver et al., J. Mol. Biol. 193, 189 (1987) [90]

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Comparison of predicted distance distributions against experimental DEER data and crystal structure validation

mode_distance_MAD_context_specific.interpretation: Mean absolute displacement between predicted and experimental distance distribution modes using context-specific rotamer libraries, consistent with monofunctional label modeling methods

average_distance_MAD_context_specific.interpretation: Mean absolute displacement between predicted and experimental average distances using context-specific rotamer libraries

average_distance_MAD_aggregate.interpretation: Mean absolute displacement between predicted and experimental average distances using aggregate rotamer library

heavy_atom_RMSD.interpretation: Root mean squared deviation between modeled and crystal structure heavy atoms (excluding β-carbons)

spin_center_deviation.interpretation: Deviation between modeled and crystal structure spin centers

computational_time_per_rotamer.interpretation: Average time per RX rotamer on Intel core i5-4670 K CPU

computational_time_per_library.interpretation: Total time for average-sized rotamer library (46 rotamers)

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The research objective was achieved. The rotamer library-based approach successfully models bifunctional RX spin labels with accuracy comparable to monofunctional label methods (MAD of 1.3 Å for mode distances), while being considerably faster than MD-based methods (33 s per library vs. extensive MD sampling). The method produces accurate distance distribution predictions from SDSL EPR experiments with reasonable computational performance, enabling quantitative application of experimental data to protein modeling.
